Front: How does a Kubernetes Service decide which pods to route traffic to?
Back: It matches its SELECTOR against pod LABELS: every pod whose labels match the Service's selector is in the pool, and the Service load-balances across them. The selector determines WHICH pods (membership); the load balancer is HOW traffic is spread. Not the scheduler, not the replica count.

Front: Name the three Kubernetes Service types and what each does.
Back: ClusterIP = default, internal-only reachable within the cluster (what Ingress routes to). NodePort = exposes the service on each node's IP at a static port. LoadBalancer = provisions an external address (cloud load balancer) to accept external requests.

Front: What is a Kubernetes namespace and give reasons to use one.
Back: A 'virtual cluster inside a cluster' for organizing resources. Reasons: (1) logical grouping of complex apps, (2) avoid team conflicts in default, (3) share resources across staging/dev in one cluster, (4) access control + resource limits (CPU/RAM/storage) per namespace. Each namespace needs its own ConfigMap/Secret; reach another namespace's service via service-name.namespace.

Front: Which Kubernetes resources are NOT namespaced?
Back: Cluster-scoped resources like Node and Volume (PersistentVolume) live globally and can't be isolated to a namespace. Check with: kubectl api-resources --namespaced=false (and =true for namespaced ones).

Front: Difference between an Ingress and an Ingress Controller?
Back: Ingress = the resource/rules you write (host/path -> internal service). Ingress Controller = the running component (e.g. nginx ingress controller pod) that reads those rules, accepts external traffic, and routes it; the cluster entry point. An Ingress resource does nothing until a controller is installed (e.g. minikube addons enable ingress).

Front: Ingress on cloud vs bare metal?
Back: Cloud providers give an out-of-the-box virtualized load balancer as the entry point. On bare metal you must configure your own entry point / proxy server with a public IP and open ports, since no server inside the cluster is externally accessible by default.

Front: What is Helm and what is a Helm chart?
Back: Helm is the package manager for Kubernetes. A chart bundles reusable, parameterized YAML manifests (values.yaml) so you can deploy a whole app (a database, an Elastic logging stack) with one command and distribute it via public/private repos, instead of hand-writing many manifests.

Front: Container With Most Water: which pointer do you move and why?
Back: Two pointers at both ends; area = width * min(left, right). Move the pointer at the SHORTER wall inward. Width only shrinks as they converge, so the only way to improve is a taller min; moving the taller wall can't help. Discarding the shorter wall is safe because it can't beat the current area with any inner wall. O(n)/O(1).
